summary: Gruber argues forcefully that Democrats should begin impeachment proceedings against Trump following the Mueller report, regardless of whether conviction in the Senate seems likely. He cites a growing chorus of sober-minded voices — including a Republican transition staffer, conservative commentators, and constitutional law scholars — who are now calling for impeachment. His central argument is that failing to impeach would make Congress as a whole complicit, while impeaching and losing in the Senate would place responsibility squarely on Republicans. He also pushes back on the assumption that Republican Senate support is immovable, comparing political erosion to Hemingway's description of bankruptcy: gradually, then suddenly.
key_points: Democrats should impeach even expecting a Senate loss, because not impeaching makes Congress as a whole complicit rather than placing blame on Senate Republicans alone
Republican support for Trump is not as immovable as assumed — political support erodes gradually then suddenly, and career Republicans are beginning to break ranks
Constitutional scholars and conservative voices are now treating impeachment as a serious constitutional obligation, not partisan overreach
Trump's own reaction — tweeting about impeachment while stonewalling oversight — reveals his worry and paradoxically moves impeachment further into the mainstream
The Mueller report establishes impeachable conduct even without criminal conviction, since Trump's obstruction failed only because subordinates refused to comply
money_quotes: If the president has committed impeachable offenses it is Congress's duty to impeach. It's that simple.
If Democrats impeach, make their case solemnly and truthfully, and Trump survives in the Senate, then it's all on one party, the Republicans.
If they do not impeach, history will judge that Trump remained in office due to the cowardice and corruption of Congress as a whole, Democrats and Republicans alike.
Republicans in the Senate will stick with Trump only until they don't.
Political support erodes similarly to how Hemingway described going bankrupt: 'Two ways. Gradually, then suddenly.'
A burst dam begins with a few cracks.
Democrats tend to overthink things, to succumb to indecision.
Ignore the noise and listen closely — the drumbeat is growing.
key_insight: Failing to impeach a president who has committed impeachable offenses implicates all of Congress in his survival, while impeaching — even without conviction — places the historical burden squarely on those who chose to protect him.
